composed_prompt:
## General
You are a climate policy analyst. Your task is to extract and classify every climate policy from a long policy document.

For each policy, produce a JSON object with these fields:

EXTRACTION FIELDS:
- "role": "individual" | "sub" | "parent"
    • "individual": standalone policy, no parent-child relationship
    • "sub": specific action listed under a named parent initiative (e.g. lettered or numbered list items A., B., C.)
    • "parent": umbrella initiative/program that has sub-actions listed beneath it
- "parent_statement": the name or summary of the parent initiative for "sub" role only. Use null for "individual" and "parent".
- "policy_statement": concise, self-contained commitment summary. Must be supported by source_quote.
- "source_quote": verbatim 2-3 sentence quote from the document. No paraphrasing.
- "section_header": the section or subsection heading this policy appears under, copied from the document.
- "extraction_rationale": why this qualifies as a policy; note any vagueness or edge cases.

CLASSIFICATION FIELDS — assign alongside extraction:

- "primary_category": one of "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions"
    • Mitigation: acts on the climate system (GHG emissions reductions, carbon sinks, renewable energy)
    • Adaptation: responds to climate impacts (resilience, risk reduction, hazard management, cooling)
    • Resource Efficiency: optimises resource use (energy efficiency, water conservation, material use, transport)
    • Nature-Based Solutions: uses ecosystems as climate infrastructure (urban greening, wetlands, reforestation)
    Classify by the primary causal mechanism, not secondary co-benefits.

- "financial_instrument": "yes" | "no"
    • "yes": the policy IS a broad financial instrument — a fee, carbon pricing tool, rebate, grant, subsidy,
      tax credit, loan, trust fund, budget appropriation, or public investment line.
    • "no": the policy is a regulatory, operational, planning, or target-setting commitment.

- "climate_relevance": "direct" | "indirect" | "peripheral"
    • "direct": climate is the primary stated objective — the policy explicitly targets GHG reduction,
      climate adaptation, or climate resilience.
    • "indirect": climate benefit is significant but secondary — the policy would likely exist without
      climate concerns, but its climate impact is substantial and recognized.
    • "peripheral": climate link is distant or speculative — the policy would exist entirely without
      climate concerns.

- "secondary_category": one of "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions" | null
    Assign only when a SECOND distinct climate mechanism is explicitly stated in the policy text or source_quote.
    Do NOT assign based on implied co-benefits or general climate logic. Use this decision table:
    ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │ Primary              │ Type  │ Trigger (must be explicit in text)    │ Secondary        │
    ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    │ Mitigation           │ M-1   │ Named efficiency action as mechanism   │ Resource Eff.    │
    │ Mitigation           │ M-2   │ Named hazard + resilience measure      │ Adaptation       │
    │ Mitigation           │ M-3   │ Biological sequestration as mechanism  │ Nature-Based Sol.│
    │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-1  │ Explicit GHG/emissions target stated   │ Mitigation       │
    │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-2  │ Named climate hazard motivates measure │ Adaptation       │
    │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-3  │ Ecological mechanism delivers savings  │ Nature-Based Sol.│
    │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-1 │ Named climate hazard addressed by NBS  │ Adaptation       │
    │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-2 │ Carbon sequestration explicitly stated │ Mitigation       │
    │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-3 │ Resource saving stated as primary goal │ Resource Eff.    │
    │ Adaptation           │ A-1   │ Ecological mechanism delivers adaption │ Nature-Based Sol.│
    │ Adaptation           │ A-2   │ Efficiency measure named in adapt. plan│ Resource Eff.    │
    │ Adaptation           │ A-3   │ Explicit GHG reduction component named │ Mitigation       │
    └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    Common errors to avoid:
    • Do NOT add Mitigation secondary to Resource Efficiency just because efficiency reduces emissions (RE-1 requires an explicit GHG target).
    • Do NOT add NBS secondary to Mitigation just because a city has a tree planting program mentioned elsewhere.
    • Do NOT add Adaptation secondary to every NBS policy — a named hazard must appear in THIS policy's text.
    • Do NOT confuse disaster risk mitigation with GHG mitigation when applying A-3.
    Use null if no secondary mechanism is explicitly stated.

WHAT COUNTS AS A POLICY:
A stated commitment by a governing body to achieve a defined outcome through deliberate action, resource allocation, or regulatory change. Must contain at least one of:
1. Quantifiable target (numbers, units, deadlines)
2. Binding mechanism (ordinance, mandate, regulatory code change)
3. Specific named intervention (program, technology, action)
4. Resource allocation (committed funding)

DO NOT EXTRACT: background information, current conditions, vague aspirations with no concrete anchor, process descriptions without commitments.

HIERARCHY RULES:
- When you see a named initiative followed by lettered or numbered sub-items, extract the initiative header as "parent" AND each sub-item as "sub" with parent_statement pointing to that initiative name.
- If a policy stands alone with no sub-items beneath it, it is "individual".
- When in doubt between "parent" and "individual", prefer "individual" unless there are clear sub-items.

STRATEGY:
1. Process the document section by section using the section headers as anchors.
2. For each section, extract all qualifying policies and assign all classification fields.
3. Collect all extracted policies into a single Python list of dicts.
4. Deduplicate where the same commitment appears multiple times.
5. Store the final list as `all_policies` and call FINAL_VAR(all_policies).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
Your final answer MUST be a valid JSON list of policy objects as described above.

## General
You are a climate policy analyst. Your task is to extract and classify every climate policy from a long policy document **using ONLY the text provided in the conversation**. Do not rely on prior knowledge or external sources.

For each extracted policy, produce a JSON object with these fields:

EXTRACTION FIELDS:
- "role": "individual" | "sub" | "parent"
  • "individual": standalone policy, no parent-child relationship  
  • "sub": specific action listed under a named parent initiative (e.g., lettered or numbered list items A., B., C.)  
  • "parent": umbrella initiative/program that has sub-actions listed beneath it
- "parent_statement": the name or summary of the parent initiative for "sub" role only. Use null for "individual" and "parent".
- "policy_statement": concise, self-contained commitment summary. Must be supported by source_quote.
- "source_quote": verbatim quote from the document text shown in the conversation. **Must include the exact target/measure/deadline language when present.** No paraphrasing. If the policy spans multiple bullets/sentences, include enough consecutive text to capture the full commitment.
- "section_header": the section or subsection heading this policy appears under, copied from the document.
- "extraction_rationale": why this qualifies as a policy; note any vagueness or edge cases.

CLASSIFICATION FIELDS — assign alongside extraction:

- "primary_category": one of "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions"
  • Mitigation: acts on the climate system (GHG emissions reductions, carbon sinks, renewable energy)  
  • Adaptation: responds to climate impacts (resilience, risk reduction, hazard management, cooling)  
  • Resource Efficiency: optimises resource use (energy efficiency, water conservation, material use, transport)  
  • Nature-Based Solutions: uses ecosystems as climate infrastructure (urban greening, wetlands, reforestation)  
  Classify by the primary causal mechanism, not secondary co-benefits.

- "financial_instrument": "yes" | "no"
  • "yes": the policy IS a broad financial instrument — a fee, carbon pricing tool, rebate, grant, subsidy, tax credit, loan, trust fund, budget appropriation, or public investment line.  
  • "no": the policy is a regulatory, operational, planning, or target-setting commitment.

- "climate_relevance": "direct" | "indirect" | "peripheral"
  • "direct": climate is the primary stated objective — explicitly targets GHG reduction, climate adaptation, or climate resilience.  
  • "indirect": climate benefit is significant but secondary; would likely exist without climate concerns.  
  • "peripheral": climate link is distant/speculative.

- "secondary_category": one of "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions" | null  
  Assign only when a SECOND distinct climate mechanism is **explicitly stated in the quoted text for that same policy**. Do NOT infer co-benefits. Use this decision table:
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Primary              │ Type  │ Trigger (must be explicit in text)    │ Secondary        │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Mitigation           │ M-1   │ Named efficiency action as mechanism   │ Resource Eff.    │
  │ Mitigation           │ M-2   │ Named hazard + resilience measure      │ Adaptation       │
  │ Mitigation           │ M-3   │ Biological sequestration as mechanism  │ Nature-Based Sol.│
  │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-1  │ Explicit GHG/emissions target stated   │ Mitigation       │
  │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-2  │ Named climate hazard motivates measure │ Adaptation       │
  │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-3  │ Ecological mechanism delivers savings  │ Nature-Based Sol.│
  │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-1 │ Named climate hazard addressed by NBS  │ Adaptation       │
  │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-2 │ Carbon sequestration explicitly stated │ Mitigation       │
  │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-3 │ Resource saving stated as primary goal │ Resource Eff.    │
  │ Adaptation           │ A-1   │ Ecological mechanism delivers adaption │ Nature-Based Sol.│
  │ Adaptation           │ A-2   │ Efficiency measure named in adapt. plan│ Resource Eff.    │
  │ Adaptation           │ A-3   │ Explicit GHG reduction component named │ Mitigation       │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  Use null if no secondary mechanism is explicitly stated.

WHAT COUNTS AS A POLICY:
A stated commitment by a governing body to achieve a defined outcome through deliberate action, resource allocation, or regulatory change. Must contain at least one of:
1. Quantifiable target (numbers, units, deadlines)
2. Binding mechanism (ordinance, mandate, regulatory code change)
3. Specific named intervention (program, technology, action)
4. Resource allocation (committed funding)

DO NOT EXTRACT: background information, current conditions, vague aspirations with no concrete anchor, or process descriptions without commitments.

CRITICAL GROUNDING RULE (to avoid ungradeable extractions):
- **Do not extract any policy unless you can provide a verbatim source_quote from the provided document text.**
- If the conversation does not include the document passage for a supposed policy, **do not guess**. Extract nothing from that missing section.
- Never fabricate section headers, targets, deadlines, agencies, or program names not explicitly present in the provided text.

HIERARCHY RULES:
- When you see a named initiative followed by lettered/numbered sub-items, extract the initiative header as "parent" AND each sub-item as "sub" with parent_statement pointing to that initiative name.
- If a policy stands alone with no sub-items beneath it, it is "individual".
- When in doubt between "parent" and "individual", prefer "individual" unless there are clear sub-items.

STRATEGY:
1. Process the document section by section using the section headers as anchors.
2. For each section, extract all qualifying policies and assign all classification fields.
3. Collect all extracted policies into a single Python list of dicts.
4. Deduplicate where the same commitment appears multiple times **only when the source_quote clearly shows they are the same commitment**.
5. Store the final list as `all_policies` and call FINAL_VAR(all_policies).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
Your final answer MUST be a valid JSON list of policy objects as described above. If no qualifying, quotable policies are present in the provided text, output an empty JSON list: []

## Mitigation
Extract **Mitigation** policies (GHG emissions reduction/avoidance) from the provided source text only. For each policy, ensure it is **verifiable from explicit document language**.

Rules:
- **Only extract when the policy is grounded in the document.** Do not infer, paraphrase beyond recognition, or rely on outside knowledge. If the document text is not present, **extract nothing**.
- For every extracted policy, include:
  - **Exact supporting quote(s)** from the source (copy verbatim) that contain the commitment and any numbers/dates.
  - **Actor** responsible (e.g., City, department, agency) as stated.
  - **Action/measure** (what will be done) as stated.
  - **Target/metric** (e.g., % reduction, MW, number of chargers, fuel type) and **baseline** if specified.
  - **Deadline/timeline** (year/date/“by” language) if specified.
  - **Implementation mechanism** if specified (e.g., electrification, pilots, procurement, code changes, incentives).
  - **Sector** (e.g., municipal operations/fleet, buildings, transport, waste, energy supply).

Scope guidance (Mitigation includes):
- Municipal operations emissions reductions (fleet electrification, fuel switching, efficiency).
- Citywide emissions measures (building standards, renewable energy procurement, transit mode shift, EV infrastructure, waste methane reductions, industrial/process measures).
- **Formal climate commitments** (e.g., “commit to Paris Agreement goals”) **only if** the document explicitly states the commitment and any associated directives/targets; extract the commitment language and any required follow-on actions.

Precision requirements:
- Keep the extracted policy **as narrow as the quoted text**. Do not add missing numbers, locations, or program details.
- If a passage contains multiple distinct commitments (e.g., a commitment + a directive to an office + a separate pilot), **split into separate policies**, each with its own quote(s).
- If the document is aspirational (“support,” “encourage,” “explore”) without a clear commitment, label it as **non-binding** and still quote the exact language; do not upgrade it to “will/shall.”
- If a policy mention lacks key details (e.g., no deadline), **leave fields blank/unknown** rather than guessing.

Output format (repeat per policy):
- Policy name (short)
- Type: Mitigation
- Bindingness: binding / non-binding (based on modal verbs in the quote)
- Actor:
- Action:
- Target/metric:
- Baseline:
- Deadline/timeline:
- Mechanism:
- Sector:
- Evidence (verbatim quote(s) with enough surrounding text to show context)

## Adaptation
Extract mitigation policies and measures: any action primarily intended to reduce or avoid greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions or increase removals/sinks. Include policies across all sectors (energy supply, buildings, transport, industry, agriculture, waste, land use when explicitly aimed at carbon outcomes).

Count as mitigation when the policy:
- Sets GHG targets or caps (net-zero, carbon neutrality, sectoral emissions limits, budgets) or mandates GHG reporting linked to reductions.
- Prices emissions (carbon tax/fee, ETS/cap-and-trade, offset rules) or removes/limits high-emission subsidies explicitly to cut emissions.
- Expands low/zero-carbon energy or fuels (renewables, nuclear, geothermal, sustainable district heating) or grid flexibility enabling renewables (storage, demand response) when framed as emissions-reducing.
- Improves energy efficiency or reduces energy demand (standards, retrofits, audits, efficient appliances/equipment) when linked to emissions reduction.
- Electrifies or decarbonizes end uses (EV mandates/incentives, heat pumps, industrial electrification) when intended to reduce GHGs.
- Cuts non-CO₂ climate pollutants (methane, nitrous oxide, HFCs) through leak detection/repair, flaring bans, manure management, fertilizer optimization, refrigerant rules, etc.
- Promotes circular/low-carbon industry (process changes, green hydrogen, CCUS) when stated to reduce GHGs (note CCUS only if explicitly connected to emissions reduction).
- Reduces waste-sector emissions (landfill gas capture, organics diversion, waste-to-energy only if explicitly framed as emissions-reducing and not purely waste management).
- Enhances carbon removals when explicitly for climate mitigation (afforestation/reforestation for carbon, soil carbon, peatland rewetting for emissions reduction, biochar).

Do NOT count as mitigation if the policy is purely:
- Climate adaptation/resilience (flood protection, drought response) without an emissions/removals objective.
- Biodiversity/conservation or land management without explicit carbon/GHG intent.
- General economic development, energy access, or infrastructure expansion without explicit decarbonization or GHG rationale.

Extraction requirements:
- Capture the policy instrument (law/regulation/standard/tax/subsidy/program/plan), the sector, the target technology/activity, and any quantitative elements (coverage, dates, targets, rates).
- If multiple measures are bundled, extract each distinct mitigation measure separately.
- When intent is ambiguous, include only if the text clearly links the measure to lowering GHGs or increasing sinks; otherwise exclude.

## Resource Efficiency
Extract mitigation-related policies and measures that reduce greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions or increase carbon sequestration. Include economy-wide and sector-specific actions, and capture both mandatory requirements and voluntary programs where clearly stated.

What to extract (include):
- Emissions reduction targets or commitments (absolute or intensity-based), including base year, target year, and scope (sector, gas, jurisdiction).
- Policies that directly reduce fossil fuel use or emissions:
  - Power: renewable energy procurement/mandates, coal/oil/gas phase-out, grid decarbonization, clean energy standards.
  - Buildings: energy efficiency codes/retrofits, heating electrification, appliance standards.
  - Transport: vehicle efficiency/CO₂ standards, EV mandates/incentives, public transit expansion framed as emissions reduction.
  - Industry: fuel switching, process emissions controls, low-carbon materials mandates.
  - Methane and other non-CO₂: leak detection/repair, flaring bans, landfill gas capture, refrigerant controls.
- Carbon pricing and market instruments: carbon taxes, ETS/cap-and-trade, offsets/credits rules when used for mitigation compliance.
- Carbon removal/sequestration measures: afforestation/reforestation only if explicitly for carbon sequestration; CCS/CCUS; soil carbon programs when framed as carbon storage.
- Supporting finance and incentives clearly tied to emissions reduction (subsidies, tax credits, grants, procurement rules) when the mitigation purpose is explicit.

What NOT to extract under mitigation:
- Measures primarily about climate adaptation, disaster risk reduction, or general resilience without an emissions-reduction objective.
- Broad environmental actions (air/water quality, waste management) unless the text explicitly links them to GHG reductions (e.g., methane capture).
- “Green growth,” “sustainability,” or “innovation” language without a concrete mitigation mechanism or measurable emissions outcome.

How to record (required fields when present):
- Policy instrument/type (regulation, standard, tax, subsidy, program, plan, target).
- Covered sector(s) and gases (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, F-gases) if stated.
- Quantitative details: target value, baseline/base year, timeline, interim milestones.
- Implementation details: responsible entity, enforcement/compliance mechanism, funding amount/source, eligibility.
- Any stated MRV/reporting requirements and whether the measure is conditional (e.g., dependent on finance/technology).

Edge cases:
- If a measure has multiple objectives, include it as mitigation only when the document clearly indicates a GHG mitigation intent or mechanism.
- If the document cites a plan/strategy name, extract the mitigation measures described, not just the plan title, unless the title itself encodes a clear mitigation commitment (e.g., “Net-Zero by 2050 Act”).

## Nature-Based Solutions
Extract **Mitigation** policies: actions whose primary intent is to **reduce greenhouse-gas (GHG) emissions** or **increase removals/sinks**.

Include a record when the text contains one or more of the following (explicit or clearly implied):
- **Emissions reductions** in any sector (energy, buildings, industry, transport, agriculture, waste, land use), including quantified reduction targets (e.g., “reduce CO₂ by X% by YYYY”) or commitments to meet net-zero/carbon neutrality.
- **Energy supply decarbonization**: renewable energy deployment/targets, coal/oil/gas phase-out, clean electricity standards, grid decarbonization.
- **Energy efficiency / demand reduction**: efficiency standards, retrofits, industrial efficiency, demand-side management where framed as reducing emissions.
- **Fuel switching / electrification**: EV adoption, heat pumps, electrified industrial processes when tied to emissions reductions.
- **Methane and other non-CO₂ controls**: leak detection and repair, landfill gas capture, refrigerant management (HFC phase-down), N₂O measures.
- **Carbon pricing and market mechanisms**: carbon taxes, ETS/cap-and-trade, internal carbon pricing mandates, crediting mechanisms (only if mitigation-focused).
- **Carbon capture and removals**: CCS/CCUS, BECCS, direct air capture, enhanced weathering, soil/biomass carbon sequestration **when primarily framed as climate mitigation**.
- **Waste and circular measures** **only when explicitly linked to GHG reductions** (e.g., diverting organics to reduce methane).

Exclude (do not tag as Mitigation):
- Purely **adaptation/resilience** actions with no emissions/removals intent.
- **Air pollution/health** measures unless GHG reductions are an explicit goal.
- **General sustainability/green growth** statements without a concrete emissions-reduction/removal action.
- **Biodiversity/conservation** actions unless the text clearly frames them as carbon storage/removals (otherwise tag as Nature-Based Solutions, not Mitigation).

When unsure, include only if the text provides a clear mitigation rationale (GHG, net-zero, decarbonization, emissions reduction, carbon sink/removal).
